and commonly known as **Glasgow 2014** were an international multi-sport event celebrated in the tradition of the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") as governed by the [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") (CGF). It took place in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), Scotland, from 23 July to 3 August 2014.

The **2014 Commonwealth Games**, officially known as the **XX Commonwealth Games** and commonly known as **Glasgow 2014** were an international multi-sport event celebrated in the tradition of the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") as governed by the [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") (CGF). It took place in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), Scotland, from 23 July to 3 August 2014.

Glasgow was selected as the host city on 9 November 2007 during CGF General Assembly in [Colombo, Sri Lanka](/wiki/Colombo "Colombo"), defeating [Abuja, Nigeria](/wiki/Abuja "Abuja"). It was the largest [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") ever held in Scotland with around 4,950 athletes from 71 different nations and territories competing in 18 different sports, outranking the [1970](/wiki/1970_British_Commonwealth_Games "1970 British Commonwealth Games") and [1986](/wiki/1986_Commonwealth_Games "1986 Commonwealth Games") Commonwealth Games in Edinburgh.

The Games received acclaim for their organisation, attendance, and the public enthusiasm of the people of Scotland, with CGF chief executive Mike Hooper hailing them as "the standout games in the history of the movement".[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Held in Scotland for the third time, the Games were notable for the successes of the [Home Nations](/wiki/Home_Nations "Home Nations") of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), with [England](/wiki/England_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "England at the 2014 Commonwealth Games"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Wales at the 2014 Commonwealth Games") and hosts [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Scotland at the 2014 Commonwealth Games") achieving their largest ever gold medal hauls and overall medal hauls at a Commonwealth Games.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) [England](/wiki/England "England") finished top of the medal table for the first time since the [1986 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1986_Commonwealth_Games "1986 Commonwealth Games"), also held in Scotland. [Kiribati](/wiki/Kiribati "Kiribati") also won its first ever medal at a Commonwealth Games, a gold in the 105 kg men's weightlifting competition.[[8]](#cite_note-8) These were the final Commonwealth Games to be opened by [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"); the final two Games of her reign, in 2018 and 2022 were opened by her son, then-Prince of Wales [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III").

## Host selection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Host selection")]

Main articles: [Bids for the 2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Bids_for_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Bids for the 2014 Commonwealth Games"), [Glasgow bid for the 2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Glasgow_bid_for_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Glasgow bid for the 2014 Commonwealth Games"), and [Abuja bid for the 2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Abuja_bid_for_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Abuja bid for the 2014 Commonwealth Games")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Dawn_over_Glasgow_%2848659596342%29.jpg/330px-Dawn_over_Glasgow_%2848659596342%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dawn_over_Glasgow_(48659596342).jpg)

[Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") was selected by the [Commonwealth Games Council for Scotland](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Council_for_Scotland "Commonwealth Games Council for Scotland") as the main Scottish candidate city for the 2014 Commonwealth Games

Scotland was the first country to consider hosting the 2014 Commonwealth Games in 2004, with Scottish cities being invited by the [Commonwealth Games Council for Scotland](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Council_for_Scotland "Commonwealth Games Council for Scotland") to consider making a bid. In September 2004, Glasgow was announced as the Scottish candidate city over [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh") (which hosted the Games in [1970](/wiki/1970_British_Commonwealth_Games "1970 British Commonwealth Games") and [1986](/wiki/1986_Commonwealth_Games "1986 Commonwealth Games"), and the inaugural [Commonwealth Youth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Youth_Games "Commonwealth Youth Games") in [2000](/wiki/2000_Commonwealth_Youth_Games "2000 Commonwealth Youth Games")) following a [cost-benefit analysis](/wiki/Cost-benefit_analysis "Cost-benefit analysis") by the Commonwealth Games Council for Scotland. The [Scottish Executive](/wiki/Scottish_Executive "Scottish Executive") under then [First Minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), [Jack McConnell](/wiki/Jack_McConnell "Jack McConnell"), with the support of the [United Kingdom government](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Government "Her Majesty's Government") and all main parties in the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), formally announced Glasgow's intention to host the games on 16 August 2005.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-BBC_final-10)

In March 2006, the bidding process began, with the [Glasgow Bidding](/wiki/Glasgow_bid_for_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Glasgow bid for the 2014 Commonwealth Games") team presenting their case to the [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") at the [2006 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games") in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), along with the other confirmed candidate cities; the Nigerian capital, [Abuja](/wiki/Abuja_bid_for_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Abuja bid for the 2014 Commonwealth Games") and [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax_bid_for_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Halifax bid for the 2014 Commonwealth Games") in Canada.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In October 2006, the first voting delegates arrived in Glasgow, to inspect the city's existing and proposed amenities and facilities. Glasgow announced on 16 January 2007, the 17 sports to be included should its bid be successful.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Halifax later withdrew its bid on 8 March 2007, following the withdrawal of funding from the municipal government.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/GlasgowSubwayCommonwealth.jpg/250px-GlasgowSubwayCommonwealth.jpg)](/wiki/File:GlasgowSubwayCommonwealth.jpg)

Special liveries in support of Glasgow's bid were applied to numerous subway carriages.

That left Abuja and Glasgow as the remaining bidders, with Abuja seen as a likely favourite due to the basis of its campaign that an African nation has never before hosted the Commonwealth Games.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The deadline for formal submission of bids to the Commonwealth Games Federation, in the form of a Candidate City File, was set for May 2007.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Both bids were highly recommended, though Glasgow's bid team had made use of extensive benchmarking against the [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games") in Manchester and the 2006 Commonwealth Games in Melbourne and as a result, its bid was deemed technically superior according to the CGF Evaluation Report that was released in September 2007. The Commonwealth Games Evaluation Commission concluded that: "Glasgow has shown it has the ability to stage the 2014 Commonwealth Games to a standard which would continue to enhance the image and prestige of the Games." This put Glasgow ahead in terms of the technical comprehensiveness of its bid.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The final decision on the host city of the 2014 Commonwealth Games was held in [Colombo](/wiki/Colombo "Colombo"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") on 9 November 2007 at the Commonwealth Games Federation General Assembly, attended by all 71 [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") Games member associations. Each bid city made a presentation to the General Assembly, the order of which was determined by drawing lots. Glasgow's delegation was led by Louise Martin, chair of the Commonwealth Games Council for Scotland, First Minister [Alex Salmond](/wiki/Alex_Salmond "Alex Salmond"), athlete Jamie Quarry and Leader of [Glasgow City Council](/wiki/Glasgow_City_Council "Glasgow City Council") [Steven Purcell](/wiki/Steven_Purcell "Steven Purcell"). The presentation also included a promotional film narrated by [Sean Connery](/wiki/Sean_Connery "Sean Connery").[[17]](#cite_note-17) Abuja's delegation was led by General [Yakubu Gowon](/wiki/Yakubu_Gowon "Yakubu Gowon"), head of the Abuja 2014 Commonwealth Games bid team.

The CGF members later voted for their preferred candidate in a secret ballot. As there were only two bids, the winner was announced by the CGF President, Mike Fennel, after the first round of voting, with the winner only requiring a simple majority. ## Development and preparation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Development and preparation")]

The Glasgow 2014 Organising Committee was responsible for organising and delivering the Games.[[18]](#cite_note-18) It was established as a limited company[[19]](#cite_note-19) whose members included the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), [Glasgow City Council](/wiki/Glasgow_City_Council "Glasgow City Council") and [Commonwealth Games Scotland](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Scotland "Commonwealth Games Scotland"). Glasgow City Council was responsible for delivering a number of the venues and lasting benefits from the Games for the city and people of Glasgow. The Scottish Government was the third organisation in the partnership and was the main funder of the Games.[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Lord Robert Smith](/wiki/Robert_Smith,_Baron_Smith_of_Kelvin "Robert Smith, Baron Smith of Kelvin") was the chairman of the Glasgow 2014 Organising Committee.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[Celtic Park](/wiki/Celtic_Park "Celtic Park") hosted for the opening ceremony of the Games.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The [Commonwealth Arena and Sir Chris Hoy Velodrome](/wiki/Commonwealth_Arena_and_Sir_Chris_Hoy_Velodrome "Commonwealth Arena and Sir Chris Hoy Velodrome") precinct, situated at [Parkhead](/wiki/Parkhead "Parkhead") in the East End of the city, hosted the [Badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") as well as [Track cycling](/wiki/Track_cycling "Track cycling").[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Glasgow Green](/wiki/Glasgow_Green "Glasgow Green") was the starting point for the free Athletics (Marathon), Cycling (Road Race) and Cycling (Time Trial) events.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Glasgow Green was the venue for [Field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") and saw the construction of a new [Glasgow Green Hockey Centre](/wiki/Glasgow_Green_Hockey_Centre "Glasgow Green Hockey Centre").[[25]](#cite_note-25) [Tollcross International Swimming Centre](/wiki/Tollcross_International_Swimming_Centre "Tollcross International Swimming Centre"), was the venue for [Swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)") events. It already had one [Olympic standard 50 metre swimming pool](/wiki/Olympic_size_swimming_pool "Olympic size swimming pool"), which was extensively upgraded, and a second 50-metre pool was added for the Games as a warm-up facility. The existing permanent [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") was increased by 1,000. Combined with additional temporary seating the venue had over 5,000 seats for the Games.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

[Ibrox Stadium](/wiki/Ibrox_Stadium "Ibrox Stadium"), in the South Side, was the venue for the [Rugby Sevens](/wiki/Rugby_Sevens "Rugby Sevens") tournament.[[27]](#cite_note-27) [Mountain biking](/wiki/Mountain_bike_racing "Mountain bike racing") was held on the [Cathkin Braes](/wiki/Cathkin_Braes "Cathkin Braes") in [Rutherglen](/wiki/Rutherglen "Rutherglen"), the Royal Burgh neighbouring the city.[[28]](#cite_note-28) [Hampden Park](/wiki/Hampden_Park "Hampden Park") hosted all the [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") events as well as the closing ceremony.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The [Scottish Exhibition and Conference Centre](/wiki/SEC_Centre "SEC Centre"), located in the West End of the city, hosted the [Wrestling](/wiki/Amateur_wrestling "Amateur wrestling"), [Judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") and [Boxing](/wiki/Amateur_boxing "Amateur boxing"), as well as the Main Press Centre and the [International Broadcast Centre](/wiki/International_Broadcast_Centre "International Broadcast Centre"), benefiting from its strategic position adjacent to the headquarters of [BBC Scotland](/wiki/BBC_Scotland "BBC Scotland") and [STV](/wiki/STV_Group "STV Group") at [Pacific Quay](/wiki/Pacific_Quay "Pacific Quay"). The [Clyde Auditorium](/wiki/SEC_Armadillo "SEC Armadillo") hosted [Weightlifting](/wiki/Powerlifting "Powerlifting"), whilst the new [OVO Hydro](/wiki/OVO_Hydro "OVO Hydro") was used for the [Gymnastics](/wiki/Gymnastics "Gymnastics") and [Netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball") events.[[30]](#cite_note-30) [Kelvingrove Park](/wiki/Kelvingrove_Park "Kelvingrove Park"), also in the city's West End, was the venue for [Bowls](/wiki/Bowls "Bowls") and has five [bowling greens](/wiki/Bowling_green "Bowling green") installed for competitive use. A comprehensive upgrade and refurbishment of the park was completed ahead of the Games.[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Scotstoun Leisure Centre](/wiki/Scotstoun_Leisure_Centre "Scotstoun Leisure Centre") hosted [Table tennis](/wiki/Table_tennis "Table tennis") and [Squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The [Shooting](/wiki/Shooting_sports "Shooting sports") competitions took place at the [Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defence_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Defence (United Kingdom)") full-bore rifle and [clay target](/wiki/Clay_target "Clay target") ranges at [Barry Buddon](/wiki/Barry,_Angus "Barry, Angus"), near [Dundee](/wiki/Dundee "Dundee"), which were also used in the [1986 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1986_Commonwealth_Games "1986 Commonwealth Games"). There were temporary ranges built for the small-bore rifle and pistol events.[[33]](#cite_note-33) [Diving](/wiki/Diving_(sport) "Diving (sport)") was held at the [Royal Commonwealth Pool](/wiki/Royal_Commonwealth_Pool "Royal Commonwealth Pool") in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), located 45 miles (72 km) to the east, which held the annual [Edinburgh Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival "Edinburgh Festival") at the same time as the 2014 Commonwealth Games.[[34]](#cite_note-34) [Strathclyde Country Park](/wiki/Strathclyde_Country_Park "Strathclyde Country Park"), beside [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_South_Lanarkshire "Hamilton, South Lanarkshire") and [Motherwell](/wiki/Motherwell "Motherwell"), hosted the Triathlon event.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Athletes' village

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Athletes' village")]

The [Athletes' Village](/wiki/Athletes%27_Village_(Glasgow_2014) "Athletes' Village (Glasgow 2014)") for the 2014 Commonwealth Games was situated on a 35-hectare (86-acre) site, in [Dalmarnock](/wiki/Dalmarnock "Dalmarnock"), [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow").[[36]](#cite_note-36) The whole project was designed by RMJM.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Primarily the site was used as accommodation for the athletes competing in the games as well as team officials from every competing nation for the duration of the games. As well as accommodation, the athletes' village also housed a retail zone, dining hall, medical facilities, gym, religious centre and recreational spaces.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Budget

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Budget")]

The total budget for the Games was £575.6 million. This figure included £472.3 million for Glasgow 2014 and £90 million for security. The Glasgow 2014 budget of £472.3 million was made up of £372 million of public money with the remainder coming from commercial income generated through sponsorship, ticket sales, broadcasting rights and merchandise sales. The budget included contingency of £66.1 million, which was made up of a £42.3 million general contingency and £23.8 million special reserves. In February 2015, it was reported that the Games was delivered £35.5 million under budget.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Ticketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ticketing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Glasgow_2014_tickets_on_sale_%289544364933%29.jpg/250px-Glasgow_2014_tickets_on_sale_%289544364933%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glasgow_2014_tickets_on_sale_(9544364933).jpg)

Launch of the tickets to Glasgow 2014 going on sale

The first round of the Games ticket sales were from 19 August to 16 September 2013.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Over two-thirds of tickets sold were priced at £25 or less. The ticket price ranges for the opening ceremony and the closing ceremony were £40-£250 and £30-£200 respectively.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) The [100m men's final](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_100_metres "Athletics at the 2014 Commonwealth Games – Men's 100 metres") event received over 100,000 applications which was the most of any event of the Games.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Boxing had the most expensive ticket of any sport in the Games whose category 1 tickets cost £120.[[46]](#cite_note-46) A total of 96% of tickets were sold.[[47]](#cite_note-47) [Ticketmaster](/wiki/Ticketmaster "Ticketmaster") was the official ticket services provider of the Games.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Medals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Medals")]

The medals for the Games were unveiled on 14 April 2014 at the [Kelvingrove Art Gallery and Museum](/wiki/Kelvingrove_Art_Gallery_and_Museum "Kelvingrove Art Gallery and Museum") in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow").[[49]](#cite_note-49) The medals were designed and hand-crafted by the renowned jeweller Jonathan Boyd and a team of specialist jewellery-makers from the prestigious [Glasgow School of Art](/wiki/Glasgow_School_of_Art "Glasgow School of Art").[[50]](#cite_note-50) The hand-turned quaichs gifted to winning athletes and the carved ceremonial podiums were all created from local woods by Glasgow-based artisan Paul Hodgkiss.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Scottish designer Kerry Nixon designed the medalbearers' dresses.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[Queen's Baton](/wiki/Queen%27s_Baton_Relay#Glasgow_2014 "Queen's Baton Relay")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Queen%27s_Baton_Relay_in_Thurso_%2814435759587%29.jpg/250px-Queen%27s_Baton_Relay_in_Thurso_%2814435759587%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen%27s_Baton_Relay_in_Thurso_(14435759587).jpg)

[Ford Kuga](/wiki/Ford_Kuga "Ford Kuga") at the rear of the Queen's Baton Relay, in Olrig Street in [Thurso](/wiki/Thurso "Thurso")

The [Queen's Baton Relay](/wiki/Queen%27s_Baton_Relay "Queen's Baton Relay") began its 190,000 km journey on 9 October 2013. The baton travelled via 70 nations and territories over 288 days before opening the games on 23 July 2014. At the [ceremony](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2014 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony"), 32 volunteers from across Scotland carried the baton around [Celtic Park Stadium](/wiki/Celtic_Park "Celtic Park") after being nominated for giving their time to developing the nation's youth through sport. The baton was then passed to [Sir Chris Hoy](/wiki/Sir_Chris_Hoy "Sir Chris Hoy"), who delivered it to President of the Commonwealth Games Federation [Prince Imran](/wiki/Prince_Imran "Prince Imran") and the [Queen](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") who then declared the games open.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

The Queen's Baton was designed by the Glasgow-based firm 4c Design.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) The BBC provided coverage of the relay. Adventurer [Mark Beaumont](/wiki/Mark_Beaumont_(cyclist) "Mark Beaumont (cyclist)") presented a series of documentaries filmed on the relay for [BBC One Scotland](/wiki/BBC_One_Scotland "BBC One Scotland"), there were also weekly updates for [BBC News](/wiki/BBC_News_(British_TV_channel) "BBC News (British TV channel)") and a BBC News website and blog written by Mark.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Transport")]

[Glasgow Airport](/wiki/Glasgow_Airport "Glasgow Airport") was used to welcome and depart the athletes and officials of the Games.[[57]](#cite_note-57) The main terminal building of [Glasgow Airport](/wiki/Glasgow_Airport "Glasgow Airport") was upgraded at a cost of £17 million.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

During the games, spectators were encouraged to use the public transport, particularly for longer journeys as they make their way to the venues of the games. The Glasgow 2014 ticket included access to local public transport services (trains, buses and Subway) to and from the venue on the day of the event. In order to access those services, the spectators had to show the valid event ticket on request. The spectators were also encouraged to walk or use bicycles instead of motor vehicles to reach the venues in order to avoid traffic jam.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

The athletes were transported in minibuses or coaches, which used, where necessary, dedicated lanes – known as the Games Route Network – to ensure they were given priority over other traffic in a similar way buses are in bus lanes. These lanes formed part of a larger dedicated Games Route Network that was also used by the people working on the Games.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Volunteering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Volunteering")]

Approximately 12,500 volunteers were hired for the Games[[61]](#cite_note-61) out of over 50,000 applicants which was more than the number who applied for [Melbourne 2006](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games") and [Manchester 2002](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games").[[62]](#cite_note-62) The army of volunteers enlisted to help during the Games were known as Clyde-siders.[[63]](#cite_note-63) The uniforms for the volunteers were created by the outdoor clothing manufacturer [Trespass](/wiki/Trespass_(clothing) "Trespass (clothing)").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Security

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Security")]

An integrated security team which involved [Police Scotland](/wiki/Police_Scotland "Police Scotland"), Glasgow 2014's Security team and private sector partners, was developed to manage the security and stewarding requirements for the Games.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) More than 17,000 individuals were involved in this operation, where over 2,000 members were from the armed forces and the rest from the Police Scotland and 17 private sector companies.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) The budget for the Games security was around £90 million.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### Opening ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Opening ceremony")]

Main article: [Opening ceremony of the 2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games_opening_ceremony "2014 Commonwealth Games opening ceremony")

The programme, which included about 2,000 performers, featured [Karen Dunbar](/wiki/Karen_Dunbar "Karen Dunbar"), [John Barrowman](/wiki/John_Barrowman "John Barrowman"), [Amy Macdonald](/wiki/Amy_Macdonald "Amy Macdonald"), [Rod Stewart](/wiki/Rod_Stewart "Rod Stewart"), [Susan Boyle](/wiki/Susan_Boyle "Susan Boyle"), [Nicola Benedetti](/wiki/Nicola_Benedetti "Nicola Benedetti"), [Julie Fowlis](/wiki/Julie_Fowlis "Julie Fowlis"), [Eric Whitacre](/wiki/Eric_Whitacre "Eric Whitacre"), and [Pumeza Matshikiza](/wiki/Pumeza_Matshikiza "Pumeza Matshikiza"), as well as a message from the International Space Station. The ceremony began with a countdown and a recorded video message from Scottish actor [Ewan McGregor](/wiki/Ewan_McGregor "Ewan McGregor"), explaining the partnership between the Games and [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF"). Following the arrival of the Queen there was a flypast by the Red Arrows display team. The venue featured the largest LED video screen in Europe, supplied by Sports Technology. Scotland's then First Minister [Alex Salmond](/wiki/Alex_Salmond "Alex Salmond") welcomed the participants and spectators, and introduced a moment of silence in memory of the Malaysia Airlines Flight 17 disaster. The final part of the [Queen's Baton Relay](/wiki/Queen%27s_Baton_Relay "Queen's Baton Relay") was run by 32 Scottish volunteers nominated for giving their time to developing the nation's youth through sport. The baton was then passed to [Sir Chris Hoy](/wiki/Chris_Hoy "Chris Hoy"), who delivered it to President of the [Commonwealth Games Federation](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Federation "Commonwealth Games Federation") [Prince Imran](/wiki/Tunku_Muda_Serting_Imran "Tunku Muda Serting Imran") and the Queen. The display of the message concealed within the baton was delayed by a difficulty in opening the device. The Games were launched in partnership with [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF"), to save and change children's lives. The unique partnership aimed "to use the power of sport to reach every child in Scotland and benefit children in every Commonwealth nation and territory." In the culmination of a groundbreaking partnership with UNICEF, the ceremony inspired millions to text donations to our shared 'Put Children First' campaign, which raised £3.5 million on the night and more than £5 million to date. The ceremony was directed by [David Zolkwer](/wiki/David_Zolkwer "David Zolkwer") with David Proctor (Executive Producer) and Sarah Gardiner (Creative Producer).[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### Closing ceremony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Closing ceremony")]

Main article: [Closing ceremony of the 2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games_closing_ceremony "2014 Commonwealth Games closing ceremony")

The closing ceremony took a visual theme of a music festival, with performers, tents, and flags within the stadium. The ceremony began with Scottish singer [Lulu](/wiki/Lulu_(singer) "Lulu (singer)") welcoming the athletes of the games. Scottish band [Deacon Blue](/wiki/Deacon_Blue "Deacon Blue") performed their signature song "Dignity". During this the workers of Glasgow were recognised as they paraded along the front of the main stand at Hampden, some on foot, others in their work vehicles. Local band Prides performed their hit song "Messiah". Speeches followed, with [Prince Imran](/wiki/Tunku_Muda_Serting_Imran "Tunku Muda Serting Imran") telling the crowd that the games were "pure dead brilliant", a local Glaswegian term. The games were officially closed and handed over to the [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast,_Queensland "Gold Coast, Queensland") for [2018](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games"), who began their own performance with Australian singer [Jessica Mauboy](/wiki/Jessica_Mauboy "Jessica Mauboy"). [Kylie Minogue](/wiki/Kylie_Minogue "Kylie Minogue") then performed a seven-hit songs set list, while the volunteer cast told the story of "a typical Glasgow night out". Her costume was designed by [Jean Paul Gaultier](/wiki/Jean_Paul_Gaultier "Jean Paul Gaultier") and headpiece designed by millinery designer Lara Jensen. The show ended with [Dougie MacLean](/wiki/Dougie_MacLean "Dougie MacLean") performing Caledonia with the other performers, and a performance of "[Auld Lang Syne](/wiki/Auld_Lang_Syne "Auld Lang Syne")". The closing ceremony was directed by David Zolkwer with David Proctor (Executive Producer) and Sarah Gardiner (Creative Producer).[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Glasgow_2014_-_Athletics_%285%29_.jpg/250px-Glasgow_2014_-_Athletics_%285%29_.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glasgow_2014_-_Athletics_(5)_.jpg)

[Athletics](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Athletics at the 2014 Commonwealth Games") at the [Hampden Park](/wiki/Hampden_Park "Hampden Park")

A total of 18 sports and 261 medal events were contested at the 2014 Commonwealth Games.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

A record 22 [para-sport](/wiki/Disabled_sports "Disabled sports") events were contested in five different sports (athletics, cycling, [lawn bowls](/wiki/Lawn_bowls "Lawn bowls"), swimming and [weightlifting](/wiki/Olympic_weightlifting "Olympic weightlifting")) and para track cycling was held for the very first time.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Commonwealth_Games_2014_Badminton_Double_Final_MAS-SGP.jpg/250px-Commonwealth_Games_2014_Badminton_Double_Final_MAS-SGP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Commonwealth_Games_2014_Badminton_Double_Final_MAS-SGP.jpg)

[Badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") was held at the [Commonwealth Arena and Sir Chris Hoy Velodrome](/wiki/Commonwealth_Arena_and_Sir_Chris_Hoy_Velodrome "Commonwealth Arena and Sir Chris Hoy Velodrome")

[Archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery") and tennis from the [2010](/wiki/2010_Commonwealth_Games "2010 Commonwealth Games") games were replaced on the sports programme with [triathlon](/wiki/Triathlon "Triathlon") (who turned back after the 2010 absence)[[150]](#cite_note-150) and judo (after eight years absence). Among sport disciplines removed from 2010 include the walking events in [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)"), [synchronised swimming](/wiki/Synchronised_swimming "Synchronised swimming") and [Greco-Roman wrestling](/wiki/Greco-Roman_wrestling "Greco-Roman wrestling"), while [mountain biking](/wiki/Mountain_biking "Mountain biking") also returned after the absence in Delhi. The number of [Shooting](/wiki/Shooting_sports "Shooting sports") medal events also dropped from 44 in 2010 to 19. Among new disciplines on the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") programme for the first time were the triathlon mixed relay event, more medal chances for women at shooting and the addition of women's [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") to the programme.[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152)

### Medal table

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Medal table")]

Main article: [2014 Commonwealth Games medal table](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games_medal_table "2014 Commonwealth Games medal table")

Only the top ten successful nations are displayed here.
The ranking in this table is consistent with [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") convention in its published medal tables. By default, the table is ordered by the number of gold medals the athletes from a nation have won (in this context, a "nation" is an entity represented by a [Commonwealth Games Association](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games_Association "Commonwealth Games Association")). The number of silver medals is taken into consideration next and then the number of bronze medals. If nations are still tied, equal ranking is given and they are listed alphabetically by their three-letter country code.[[153]](#cite_note-BBCSportResults-153)[[154]](#cite_note-G2014Medals-154)

Two bronze medals were awarded in [boxing](/wiki/Boxing_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Boxing at the 2014 Commonwealth Games"), [judo](/wiki/Judo_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Judo at the 2014 Commonwealth Games") and [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games "Wrestling at the 2014 Commonwealth Games"), except for [Women's freestyle 75 kg](/wiki/Wrestling_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_freestyle_75_kg "Wrestling at the 2014 Commonwealth Games – Women's freestyle 75 kg") as only five competitors were entered in the event. Additionally, two bronze medals were awarded in the [men's 100 m backstroke](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_100_metre_backstroke "Swimming at the 2014 Commonwealth Games – Men's 100 metre backstroke") and [women's pole vault](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_pole_vault "Athletics at the 2014 Commonwealth Games – Women's pole vault") as a result of a tie between two athletes. No bronze medal was awarded in the [men's synchronized 10 metre platform](/wiki/Diving_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_synchronised_10_metre_platform "Diving at the 2014 Commonwealth Games – Men's synchronised 10 metre platform") as only four teams competed in the event. Therefore, the total number of bronze medals is greater than the total number of gold or silver medals.

### Bid and interim logo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Bid and interim logo")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Glasgow_2014_flower_bed_-_geograph.org.uk_-_548204.jpg/250px-Glasgow_2014_flower_bed_-_geograph.org.uk_-_548204.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glasgow_2014_flower_bed_-_geograph.org.uk_-_548204.jpg)

Glasgow 2014 flower bed in the grounds of [Kelvingrove Art Gallery and Museum](/wiki/Kelvingrove_Art_Gallery_and_Museum "Kelvingrove Art Gallery and Museum"), promoting Glasgow's bid to host the 2014 Commonwealth Games.

The interim logo for the Games was first used during Glasgow's bid, with the "Candidate City" section removed following 9 November 2007, when the bid was approved. The logo depicts two [sprinters](/wiki/Sprint_(running) "Sprint (running)") woven into a [tartan](/wiki/Tartan "Tartan") motif, representing [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). The logo also vaguely resembles the [Clyde Auditorium](/wiki/Clyde_Auditorium "Clyde Auditorium"), one of Glasgow's most recognisable landmarks. The pattern, forming the [Roman numerals](/wiki/Roman_numerals "Roman numerals") XX, also represents the 20th edition of the Commonwealth Games. The text is more specifically Glaswegian, with its stylised [Mackintosh](/wiki/Charles_Rennie_Mackintosh "Charles Rennie Mackintosh") font.
A flag featuring the logo was used extensively during the bid process. The flag was flown above Merchant House in [George Square](/wiki/George_Square "George Square") daily.[[157]](#cite_note-157)

[![Commonwealth Games mural at the Kingston Bridge](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Commonwealth_Games_mural%2C_Kingston_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1748308.jpg/250px-Commonwealth_Games_mural%2C_Kingston_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1748308.jpg)](/wiki/File:Commonwealth_Games_mural,_Kingston_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1748308.jpg)

Murals painted in the buildings of Glasgow

The full Games brand identity was developed by Glasgow design studio Tangent Graphic, the lead creative agency between 2010 and 2014. Tangent's first major project was the official sport Pictograms, launched on 23 July 2011, and they continued to deliver and influence every aspect of the Glasgow 2014 identity.[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160) Tangent inherited the official logo which was designed by Marque Creative.[[161]](#cite_note-161) The logo was unveiled on [Commonwealth Day](/wiki/Commonwealth_Day "Commonwealth Day"), 8 March 2010.[[162]](#cite_note-162) It was inspired by three factors, time, data and measurement. Its rings are proportioned to represent the 20th Commonwealth Games, across 17 sports, over 11 days in 1 city. An animated version of the logo has also been produced.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

There is also a version of the logo in [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic"). Arthur Cormack, the Chair of [Bòrd na Gàidhlig](/wiki/B%C3%B2rd_na_G%C3%A0idhlig "Bòrd na Gàidhlig"), made the following official statement:

> Bòrd na Gàidhlig welcomes the Gaelic version of the logo for the Glaschu 2014 Commonwealth Games and we have been happy to work with the Glaschu 2014 team in helping them develop their identity. Given the unique importance of Gaelic to Scotland and the many Scots in the diaspora throughout the Commonwealth, we believe it should be seen, heard and spoken as widely as possible.
>
> Given the worldwide interest there will be in the Games when they take place in Glasgow, a city with a large number of Gaelic speakers, we believe they offer an exciting opportunity for Gaelic to be seen and, we hope, heard and appreciated in an international setting. We hope this is just the start; we wish the Games well and look forward to working further with Glaschu 2014 to enhance the status of Gaelic within this hugely significant event.[[164]](#cite_note-164)

The official website was built in phases, delivered by Dog Digital and Blonde.[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166) The Scottish art firm Artpistol were commissioned by the Glasgow 2014 Organising Committee to paint a series of murals across Glasgow to help celebrate and promote the Games.[[167]](#cite_note-167)

#### The Big G

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: The Big G")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/The_Big_G_2014_Commonwealth_Games.jpg/250px-The_Big_G_2014_Commonwealth_Games.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Big_G_2014_Commonwealth_Games.jpg)

The 'Big G' was set up two months before the Games in [George Square](/wiki/George_Square "George Square")

The Big G is a standalone stage-set that was situated in [George Square](/wiki/George_Square "George Square"), [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"). It was unveiled in May 2014; with Glasgow City Council leader [Gordon Matheson](/wiki/Gordon_Matheson "Gordon Matheson") commenting that it provided an "opportunity for everyone – residents, visitors and spectators – to be part of the Games". British designing firm Evolve designed the Big G.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Mascot")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Clyde_%28Mascot%29.jpg/250px-Clyde_%28Mascot%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clyde_(Mascot).jpg)

Mascot sculpture in the [Glasgow Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Glasgow_Botanic_Gardens "Glasgow Botanic Gardens")

Main article: [Clyde (mascot)](/wiki/Clyde_(mascot) "Clyde (mascot)")

Clyde, an anthropomorphic [thistle](/wiki/Thistle "Thistle") named after the [river](/wiki/River_Clyde "River Clyde") which flows through the centre of [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), was the official mascot of the 2014 Commonwealth Games. The mascot was designed by Beth Gilmour, who won a competition run by Glasgow 2014 for children to design the Mascot. Beth's drawing was then brought to life by digital agency Nerv, who turned it into a commercial character, created a full backstory, gave it a name – Clyde – and created a website for him. Clyde was finally revealed in a seven-minute animated film created by Nerv at a ceremony at [BBC Scotland](/wiki/BBC_Scotland "BBC Scotland")'s headquarters in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"). The organiser, Glasgow 2014, said the mascot's design was chosen because of its "Scottish symbolism and Glaswegian charm and likeability".[[169]](#cite_note-169)

25 life-size Clyde statues were erected at places of public interest across the city including the [Glasgow Botanic Gardens](/wiki/Glasgow_Botanic_Gardens "Glasgow Botanic Gardens"), the [Kelvingrove Art Gallery](/wiki/Kelvingrove_Art_Gallery "Kelvingrove Art Gallery") and at [George Square](/wiki/George_Square "George Square"). However following vandalism at a statue in the [Govan](/wiki/Govan "Govan") area of the city, the statues were taken down. They are expected to be re-erected in secure areas. By the final day of the Games, over 50,000 Clyde mascot [cuddly toys](/wiki/Cuddly_toy "Cuddly toy") had been sold.[[170]](#cite_note-170)

Due to popularity in the city, the Clyde mascots are currently proposed official mascots of the [Glasgow City Council](/wiki/Glasgow_City_Council "Glasgow City Council").

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Controversies")]

### Drug doping and testing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2014_Commonwealth_Games&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Drug doping and testing")]

Nigeria's [Chika Amalaha](/wiki/Chika_Amalaha "Chika Amalaha") failed a doping test and was stripped of a gold medal in the [women's 53 kg weightlifting](/wiki/Weightlifting_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_53_kg "Weightlifting at the 2014 Commonwealth Games – Women's 53 kg").[[171]](#cite_note-171) In the [women's 400 metres final](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_2014_Commonwealth_Games_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_400_metres "Athletics at the 2014 Commonwealth Games – Women's 400 metres"), Botswana's [Amantle Montsho](/wiki/Amantle_Montsho "Amantle Montsho") placed fourth; she was subsequently provisionally suspended pending the results of a B sample after failing a doping test.[[172]](#cite_note-Montsho_suspended-172) Montsho's B sample was reported as positive on 14 August 2014.[[173]](#cite_note-Montsho_B_sample-173)
